---
title: "MATH 201: Lecture 5a Handout"
subtitle: "4.1 Normal Distirbution Continued"
format: 
  pdf:
    echo: false
    message: false
    warning: false
    geometry:
      - top=0.5in
---

```{r}
library(tidyverse)
options(scipen = 999)
```

\newcommand{\blank}{\rule{2.5cm}{0.15mm}}

\vspace*{-2cm}

Name:\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_ Date:\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

## Learning goals for today

By the end of this lecture, you should be able to:

- Explain how z-scores standardize values.
- Interpret area under a normal curve as probability.
- Calculate left-tail, right-tail, and between probabilities.
- Identify and interpret unusually large or small values.
- Translate probability statements into statistical context.

## Quick Review: Normal Distribution

:::: {layout="[0.6, 0.4]"}
::: {#firstcol}
A normal distribution is:

- Symmetric
- Bell-shaped
- Defined by mean (μ) and standard deviation (σ)
- Total area under curve = 1

:::

::: {#seondcol}
```{r}
#| fig-align: center
#| fig-height: 1
#| fig-width: 1.5
# Create x values
x <- seq(-4, 4, length.out = 500)

# Standard normal density
df <- data.frame(
  x = x,
  y = dnorm(x)
)

ggplot(df, aes(x = x, y = y)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.3) +

  # Only one tick at 0 labeled mu
  # scale_x_continuous(
  #   breaks = 0,
  #   labels = "",
  #   expand = c(0.02, 0.02)
  # ) +
  
  # Remove y-axis ticks and labels
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = NULL) +
  
  theme_classic(base_size = 14) +
  theme(
    axis.title = element_blank(),
    axis.text.y = element_blank(),
    axis.ticks.y = element_blank(),
    axis.line.y = element_blank(),
    axis.ticks.x = element_blank(),
    axis.text.x = element_blank()
  )
```

:::
::::

We can approximate probabilities using:

- Empirical Rule (68–95–99.7)
- Or calculate them more precisely using z-scores (covered today!)

If $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma)$, what is a Z-score?
$$Z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$$

\vspace{2cm}

If ACT scores follow a normal distribution with mean 21 and standard deviation 6:

- Compute and interpret the Z-score for someone that scores a 24.

\vspace{3cm}

- Compute and interpret the Z-score for someone that scores a 19.

\vspace{3cm}

- Which score is more unusual, and why?

\vspace{3cm}

## Area under the curve = Probability

Let $X$ represent an ACT score. Shade the area under the curve corresponding to each case, and make a guess at the probability for each case.

```{r}
#| fig-align: center
#| fig-height: 1.5
#| fig-width: 6
# Create x values
x <- seq(3, 39, length.out = 500)

# Standard normal density
df <- data.frame(
  x = x,
  y = dnorm(x, mean = 21, sd = 6)
)

act_curve <- ggplot(df, aes(x = x, y = y)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.3) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    breaks = c(6, 9, 12, 15, 18, 21, 24, 27, 30, 33, 36),
    labels = c("", 9, "", 15, "", 21, "", 27, "", 33, "")
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = NULL) +
  theme_classic() +
  theme(
    axis.title = element_blank(),
    axis.text.y = element_blank(),
    axis.ticks.y = element_blank(),
    axis.line.y = element_blank()
    #axis.ticks.x = element_blank()
    #axis.text.x = element_blank()
  ) 

gridExtra::grid.arrange(
  act_curve + 
    labs(title = "P(X < 15)"),
  act_curve + 
    labs(title = "P(X > 15)"),
  act_curve  + 
    labs(title = "P(15 < X < 27)"),
  nrow = 1
)
```

\vspace{2cm}

How do we get those areas/probabilities exactly? **We can use R for calculations!**
  
The `pnorm()` function will calculate $P(X < x)$, the probability of being less than a value.

```{r}
#| echo: true
pnorm(12, mean = 21, sd = 6)
```

Calculate the exact probabilities you approximated above using `pnorm()`.

There are a lot of different software applications we can use to do R calculations. I recommend using OneCompiler <https://onecompiler.com/r>

![](img/onecompiler.png){width=600 height=350}

Example: National Center of Education Statistics says that in 2023 national SAT scores had a mean of 1028 and a standard deviation of 221. Assuming that the normal distribution is a good model for SAT scores, for each scenario:

- shade the corresponding region under the curve, and
- calculate the probability and write the `pnorm()` call you used.

```{r}
#| fig-align: center
#| fig-height: 1.5
#| fig-width: 6
# Create x values
x <- seq(365, 1691, length.out = 500)

# Standard normal density
df <- data.frame(
  x = x,
  y = dnorm(x, mean = 1028, sd = 221)
)

act_curve <- ggplot(df, aes(x = x, y = y)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.3) +
  # scale_x_continuous(
  #   breaks = c(6, 9, 12, 15, 18, 21, 24, 27, 30, 33, 36),
  #   labels = c("", 9, "", 15, "", 21, "", 27, "", 33, "")
  # ) +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = NULL) +
  theme_classic() +
  theme(
    axis.title = element_blank(),
    axis.text.y = element_blank(),
    axis.ticks.y = element_blank(),
    axis.line.y = element_blank()
    #axis.ticks.x = element_blank()
    #axis.text.x = element_blank()
  ) 

gridExtra::grid.arrange(
  act_curve + 
    labs(title = "P(X < 1000)"),
  act_curve + 
    labs(title = "P(X > 1500)"),
  act_curve  + 
    labs(title = "P(900 < X < 1300)"),
  nrow = 1
)
```

\vspace{1.5cm}

Example: Now assume we have a variable $X \sim N(\mu = 0, \sigma = 1)$, for each scenario:

- shade the corresponding region under the curve, and
- calculate the probability and write the `pnorm()` call you used.

```{r}
#| fig-align: center
#| fig-height: 1.5
#| fig-width: 6
# Create x values
x <- seq(-3, 3, length.out = 500)

# Standard normal density
df <- data.frame(
  x = x,
  y = dnorm(x, mean = 0, sd = 1)
)

act_curve <- ggplot(df, aes(x = x, y = y)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.3) +
  # scale_x_continuous(
  #   breaks = c(6, 9, 12, 15, 18, 21, 24, 27, 30, 33, 36),
  #   labels = c("", 9, "", 15, "", 21, "", 27, "", 33, "")
  # ) +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = NULL) +
  theme_classic() +
  theme(
    axis.title = element_blank(),
    axis.text.y = element_blank(),
    axis.ticks.y = element_blank(),
    axis.line.y = element_blank()
    #axis.ticks.x = element_blank()
    #axis.text.x = element_blank()
  ) 

gridExtra::grid.arrange(
  act_curve + 
    labs(title = "P(X < -0.1267)"),
  act_curve + 
    labs(title = "P(X > 2.1357)"),
  act_curve  + 
    labs(title = "P(-0.579 < X < 1.231)"),
  nrow = 1
)
```


\vspace{1.5cm}

If $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma)$, then $Z \sim N(0, 1)$. We call this, $N(0, 1)$, a **standard normal**.

We've learned if we know $\mu$ and $\sigma$ for a variable with a normal distribution,

- we can calculate probabilities given values $x$, using `pnorm()`,
- we can also calculate values $x$, given probabilities, using `qnrom()`!

**Percentiles** are the percent of observations that fall below a given value. For example, the median is the 50th percentile.

Example: Suppose I take the SAT and score at the 75th percentile, what is my score? What percent of people scored higher than me?

\vspace{7cm}


Example: Suppose I'm extra competitive and want to score in the top 10% of all SAT scores, what is the minimum score I can get?
